data.title: How ‘housewives heartthrob’ Moses Chan became one of Hong Kong’s top TV stars
data.description:
Moses Chan Ho is one of the most recognisable stars on television in Hong Kong, having appeared in TV hits such as War and Beauty (2004) and Heart of Greed (2007). These days, he is also the face of countless commercial services and products, ranging from private banking to health supplements.
The 54-year-old’s career is built on a foundation of professionalism and quiet dedication, reflecting a steady climb rather than a meteoric rise. It has also led the celebrated actor into the coffee world, where he has become an entrepreneur.
Chan’s early life hinted at a different future. Born in 1971 into a well-to-do family, his path seemed set when he moved to Brisbane, Australia, and enrolled in university to study accounting.
His foray into the world of entertainment began not in front of a camera, but on the runway, when he was scouted to be a model for Esprit after his graduation. This led him back to Hong Kong, where he starred in his first film, cult classic Twenty Something (1994). However, his cinematic career sputtered, and a series of minor, forgettable roles followed.

The turning point came in 2000 with his move to the Hong Kong broadcasting company TVB, where he quickly became a familiar face, landing big roles in a string of critically and commercially successful series.
“I’d only played gangsters before my first role as a doctor on [2000 series] Healing Hands II. I remember I kept walking into scenes with my hands in my pockets to act cool,” Chan tells the Post.
From the historical drama Where the Legend Begins (2002) to the palace intrigue War and Beauty (2004) to the family feud drama The Gem of Life (2008), Chan showed a remarkable range.
It was his role as the flawed but charismatic Cao Pi in Where the Legend Begins that first made audiences and critics sit up and take notice, earning him his first acting award in 2002 and the moniker of “see lai killer” – a heartthrob for housewives.
The role, as the eldest son of the legendary warlord Cao Cao during the Three Kingdoms period (220-280), is also Chan’s most memorable.
“I was a bunch of nerves and had like 20 to 30 retakes,” he recalls. “I really have to thank [fellow actors] Ada [Choi Siu-fun] and Steven [Ma Chun-wai] for being so patient. I look back on this show with the fondest memories for not only the actors but also the production and the script.”
The year 2007 marked a watershed moment for Chan when he was named best actor at the TVB Anniversary Awards for his portrayal of Teddy in the family blockbuster Heart of Greed. That same night, he also won the “My Favourite Male Character” award, achieving a rare “Double Crown” victory. Subsequent acting wins around the region led to the expansion of the title to “Quadruple Crown” holder.
Asked whether he prefers historical or modern dramas, he says he would choose historical ones.
“I’m old-fashioned that way. I think anyone who enjoys acting would feel there are similarities between the historical dramas and Shakespeare’s works. The contrast is not in the plot but rather in the nuances, the way the characters are developed … there is just more space to play with the characters.
“In contrast, modern dramas require a more natural and sincere approach. You can be a lot more dramatic in historical dramas.”
Chan’s long filming hours have not only produced an accomplished actor, but also a coffee aficionado. In an effort to better understand the craft, Chan became a certified barista and coffee taster, dedicating himself to learning everything from roasting to brewing.
In 2018, he co-founded Hong Kong coffee brand Blooms Coffee, aiming to serve high-quality brew made with ethically sourced beans.
He credits his passion for coffee to his upbringing in Australia.
“I loved the coffee culture when I lived in Australia,” he says. “I inherited the slower pace and laid-back attitude from growing up there. From drinking coffee to making my own coffee to paying attention to the brewing and bean roasting process all accumulated to my venture into the coffee industry.”

Today, Chan is known as a consummate professional and a devoted family man, something that endears him to the public. His marriage to actress Aimee Chan Yan-mei is a central part of his life, as are their three children.
“A home is happier with children,” Chan explains. “I always wanted to be a father. I thought I would be a father in my thirties, but it didn’t happen until 10 years later.”
Asked if he had any regrets about being an older father, he says it all worked out for him.
“A man in his 20s doesn’t know what he’s doing,” he says. “By their mid-thirties, things usually start to settle down, and they find their life course. If they’re lucky, they will find someone to marry and have kids, and by their forties, they will concentrate on their family and career, and hopefully they can enjoy their golden years in their fifties.”
Ultimately, it is family, not show business, that is firmly anchored at the centre of Chan’s life.
“I found that I got married when I found the direction in my life; it made me a better provider, and I can take care of my family better,” he says.
“If I hadn’t figured out who I was, it would have been very hard for me to take up the responsibility. Of course, as an older father, I find it harder to keep up with their inexhaustible energy, but anything I do with my kids, whether it’s swimming or cycling, it’s just pure happiness.”

data.title: South Korea, Thailand emerge as top choice for Chinese tourists amid Japan tensions
data.description:
With rising tensions between Beijing and Tokyo disrupting travel plans, Chinese tourists are actively seeking alternative destinations for their winter getaways. While South Korea hopes to capitalise on this diverted tourist flow, it faces fierce competition from popular hotspots like Thailand, Russia and other destinations, according to travel industry officials.
The diplomatic spat began when Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi suggested potential military intervention in the Taiwan Strait, prompting Beijing to issue a travel warning on November 14.
Chinese airlines were instructed to offer refunds on Japan-bound flights through the end of the year, leaving hundreds of thousands of Chinese travellers scrambling to adjust their plans.
The timing aligns with South Korean airlines’ efforts to expand routes to China, which are more profitable than routes to Japan. Korean Air Lines increased its Incheon-Fuzhou route from three weekly flights to four last month, and Asiana Airlines is set to operate 165 weekly flights to China by March, reflecting a 20 per cent increase in capacity.
Chinese cruise operators are also scrambling to bypass Japanese ports, opting instead to extend their stays at destinations such as Jeju Island.

Data from Chinese travel platform Qunar revealed that for the weekend of November 15, South Korea emerged as the most popular overseas destination for Chinese tourists, overtaking Japan, which previously held the top spot.
It also ranked first in flight ticket payments and search volume, followed by Thailand, Hong Kong, Malaysia, Singapore, Vietnam and Indonesia.
According to data from the Korea Tourism Organisation, 4.7 million Chinese tourists visited South Korea between January and October this year, exceeding the total number of visitors for last year.
But even as Korea hopes to benefit from the lingering tensions between China and Japan, several travel agencies said that Chinese tourists have an abundance of alternative travel destinations, even as interest in visiting South Korea continues to grow.
Some Chinese travellers are increasingly opting for destinations that are visa-free, such as Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, Georgia and Egypt, according to Li Xiaoya, a Beijing-based travel agent.
Most group tours’ flights to Japan have been cancelled, and we’re instead promoting packages for other countries and regions
Li Xiaoya, Beijing-based travel agent
“Most group tours’ flight tickets to Japan have been cancelled, and we’re now instead promoting packages for other countries and regions, such as Thailand, Semporna [in Malaysia], South Korea and others,” she said.
According to data released by Tongcheng Travel on Monday, hotel bookings by Chinese tourists for trips to South Korea in the last two weeks of November surged by over 240 per cent year-on-year.
Hotel bookings for Southeast Asian destinations such as Vietnam and Indonesia also saw year-on-year growth exceeding 100 per cent, while bookings for Germany and Spain showed an impressive year-on-year increase of more than 300 per cent.
Russia is also favoured by Chinese, thanks to the implementation of visa-free policies for up to 30 days starting this month. Chinese media reported that searches and bookings for trips to Russia surged following the announcement of the visa waiver.
Data released by Qunar last week revealed that Thailand has become the top destination for Chinese outbound tourists during the January 15 to February 10, 2026, winter holiday period. Meanwhile, Russia has experienced a significant surge in popularity, with flight bookings increasing 1.5 times year-on-year.
A staff member at a travel agency catering to Chinese tourists in South Korea noted a slight uptick in winter bookings following the China-Japan tensions. However, the increase has been modest, likely because January and February are not peak travel months for South Korea, he said.
“There are numerous options for Chinese tourists looking to experience winter landscapes, including domestic destinations like Harbin or international locations such as Russia and various European countries. South Korea is facing stiff competition in this area,” the staff member said.

A sales representative from a Shanghai-based Chinese airline revealed that the carrier initially planned to reduce flight capacity to Thailand in November and December. However, due to the ongoing tensions with Japan, they decided to increase capacity instead.
“Destinations like Sanya [in Hainan province], and Thailand usually experience some year-on-year growth among Chinese tourists during the winter season,” the representative added.
Meanwhile, South Korea remains an appealing destination for travellers from cities like Shanghai.
Willow Zhao, a 27-year-old doctoral student in Shanghai who recently cancelled her trip to Japan, said she is actively considering South Korea due to its convenient location and the allure of duty-free shopping.
However, South Korea’s visa policy may deter individual tourists adjusting to sudden travel plan changes. Currently, only group tours qualify for visa-free access. Xie Lumei, an accountant from Fujian province, said that she initially wanted to visit Seoul but decided against it, unwilling to deal with the hassle of a visa application.
This story was first published by The Korea Times

data.title: Why a Brazilian ‘K-drama oppa’ dating service is being investigated for sexual exploitation
data.description:
A dating platform in Brazil that markets paid encounters with Korean men styled as K-drama “oppa” is under investigation for sexual exploitation, with the South Korean consulate in Sao Paulo appealing for potential victims to come forward.
KDramaDate advertises itself as a service that lets women “relive the magic of K-dramas”, offering curated outings with Korean men at cafes, barbecue restaurants and popular city landmarks. One package includes what the site describes as an “intimate experience” in a motel or at the client’s home.
The consulate confirmed in late October that it had identified victims linked to the platform and had escalated its inquiry from suspected fraud to potential sexual crimes, according to notices published on its social media channels and reported on by The Korea Herald.
Investigators from the Sao Paulo state prosecutor’s office and local police are now examining the case.

KDramaDate’s website presents a carefully staged fantasy built around familiar K-drama tropes. Its homepage features couples posed in stylised scenes alongside a slogan that reads in Portuguese: “Reviva a Magia dos K-Dramas com seu Oppa!” (“Relive the magic of K-dramas with your oppa!”), a term that means “older brother” but is also used by Korean women to address older men in their social circle, often with romantic overtones.
The site advertises four packages, including cafe tours in the Koreatown district of Bom Retiro, a Korean barbecue dinner in which the “oppa” grills meat “like in the dramas”, and a walk in Ibirapuera Park where clients can request the men to recite lines from popular series.
The fourth option, labelled the “K-Experiencia Intima”, promises a private meeting “in a motel or at your residence”, accompanied by a sensual massage. Prices for the various packages are not listed on the website.
The man featured most prominently on the platform, marketed as “Oppa Rick”, is described as a trilingual or quadrilingual model with Japanese and Korean roots who can “bring K-drama scenes to life” in Sao Paulo.
Brazilian outlets including Gazeta Brazil have identified him as 23-year-old Japanese national Rikito Morikawa, who allegedly sought to recruit younger Korean men from the local community to join the project.
According to these reports, his lawyer has not been able to reach him for two months, after Morikawa indicated he would return to Japan to pursue similar work.

In a statement quoted by The Korea Herald on Thursday, Kim In-ho, the consular officer for police affairs, said authorities were doing their utmost “not only to protect Korean nationals in Brazil but also to safeguard the rights of anyone who could be harmed by those who unlawfully exploit Korea’s image or culture. We will respond firmly to any acts that undermine the cultural values of Korea”.
The consulate’s warnings have since been echoed by Brazilian Korean community groups and K-drama fan pages, some of which now post public-service advisories urging “dorameiras” – female drama fans – not to book the service.
Several media outlets, citing the consulate’s findings, have reported that authorities are also examining whether Korean men recruited by the operator may have been trafficked or exposed to coercive conditions.
Brazil is known for having one of Latin America’s largest Korean pop culture fan communities, often described as part of the global hallyu or Korean wave phenomenon encompassing K-pop, television dramas and films.
According to the Korean Cultural Centre in Sao Paulo, the country had more than 300,000 Hallyu fans as of 2019. Sao Paulo’s sizeable Korean community, the largest in the region, has long served as a magnet for fans seeking immersive cultural experiences.

The controversy surrounding KDramaDate echoes similar incidents in which scammers exploited K-drama fans in Brazil and elsewhere.
In 2022, the Korean consulate in Sao Paulo issued a warning after a Brazilian woman who was a fan of K-dramas and K-pop was scammed by someone pretending to be actor Park Bo-gum, who is known for K-dramas such as Reply 1988.
Brazilian outlets reported that the profile had tricked at least seven women, including an elderly fan, into sending money after the impostor promised to visit Brazil if she covered his travel costs.
In the United States, a widow in North Carolina lost more than US$67,000 in 2024 after believing she was communicating with K-drama star Ahn Hyo-seop from the series Dr Romantic.
In October, the agency of Squid Game actor Lee Jung-jae issued a global alert after impostors defrauded a woman of nearly 500 million won (US$340,000), stressing that the actor would “under no circumstances” request money from fans.

data.title: 140 private doctors offering free basic healthcare to Hong Kong fire victims
data.description:
More than 140 private doctors in Hong Kong have signed up to offer free basic healthcare for survivors of last week’s deadly blaze that tore through seven of eight blocks in a housing complex in Tai Po.
The government said on Thursday that the scheme spanned doctors across 170 locations, including four private hospitals. The policy allows victims to have three consultations, plus medication, free of charge between now and February 28 next year.
The scheme will be coordinated by project managers stationed at the government’s district health centres. Those in need can seek help by visiting the facilities in person or by phoning in.
Available services include prescription refills based on medical history, as well as follow-ups for trauma, respiratory illnesses and stress or anxiety resulting from the tragedy.
The Hospital Authority earlier announced that it would provide outpatient services at family medicine clinics for free to affected residents. Such facilities handled more than 300 visits by victims of the blaze, according to the government.
Medical stations at different shelters set up by the government’s Primary Healthcare Commission will operate until the shelters close and have already provided medical and psychological support services for more than 2,000 visitations.
Additional medical help includes HK$50,000 (US$6,400) being given out to each affected resident who has spent up to six days in hospital, or HK$100,000 for seven days or more. The funds will be distributed to families by their designated social workers.
The injured will also have their medical fees waived for the entire duration of their treatment and rehabilitation.
All residents of Wang Fuk Court, including domestic helpers, will be eligible for full medical fee waivers until December 21, 2026.
At least 159 people were killed in a fire that raged through the housing complex for 43 hours. Another 79 people were injured, including 12 firefighters.

data.title: World’s first ‘ethical AI’ wades into thorny debate over China’s new drug abuse law
data.description:
A new Chinese AI system called Wen Dao – billed as the world’s first such model to be designed explicitly for ethical risk detection – has landed in the middle of its first major moral dilemma.
Its immediate challenge is to navigate one of 2025’s most explosive social debates: whether the country should automatically seal the records of convicted drug users under the newly revised Public Security Administration Punishments Law.
Effective January 1, an amendment to the law known as Article 136 will mandate the sealing of records of administrative penalties for drug use, barring access by schools and employers.
The change has been framed by supporters as rehabilitation but critics have condemned it and warned that the leniency it provides risks weakening anti-drug enforcement and invokes painful historical memories of the Opium War.
Enter Wen Dao – an artificial intelligence model unveiled last month, which has been trained on classical ethical theories and a curated database of China’s judicial precedents.
Prompted with the question, “Is it ethically justified to automatically seal the personal information of individuals penalised for drug use?”, the model delivered this response: “It is advisable to adopt a conditional approach to record sealing – one that weighs individual rehabilitation against public safety and institutional accountability.
“Blindly sealing records without evaluating recidivism risk, treatment completion or social impact may inadvertently prioritise privacy over collective well-being, thereby undermining the preventive function of law,” it added.
In general, the AI concluded that while the intent behind the law may have been humane, its sweeping application has raised serious ethical concerns.


The assessment stood in stark contrast to the cautious neutrality of mainstream large language models, many of which decline to offer ethical stances on active legislation. “I cannot take a position on formal legal regulations,” was the response from DeepSeek, one of China’s most popular AI platforms, when given the same query.
Wen Dao was designed by a cross-disciplinary team at Southeast University in Nanjing, led by Professor Wang Yu, director of the AI Ethics Laboratory. The model was designed to integrate principles from Confucian ethics, utilitarianism and Chinese legal philosophy.
Its knowledge base includes landmark cases on drug policy, public health ethics and social stigma, enabling it to engage in normative reasoning rather than mere factual retrieval.
“The introduction of a policy will affect certain individuals; could it lead to injustice for some groups? AI ethical large models can provide answers to such questions,” Wang said at a press conference on November 12, according to a report by the Nanjing Morning Post.
Wen Dao was jointly developed by the National Mobile Communications Research Laboratory at Southeast University, the Moral Development Institute in Jiangsu province and a mobile information and security science centre under the Ministry of Education.
The AI model is available for free to the public, but the approach may also introduce new risks related to oversight or censorship.
Wen Dao’s developers said it was designed to fully align with China’s domestic laws and regulations, serving as an intelligent assistant for government policy formulation, regulatory ethical review, corporate compliance development and public science education.
During testing by the South China Morning Post, Wen Dao’s workflow was observed using large language models, though the specific underlying models could not be identified. The time displayed for knowledge base retrieval was 3.3 times longer than that of a large language model – 8.69 seconds vs 2.21 seconds.
“Building upon a general large model foundation, we have infused Wen Dao with classic ethical works, significant ethical and legal cases from China, as well as ethical norms derived from this year’s societal hot topics,” the model’s developer, Miao Yujie, from the School of Information Science and Engineering at Southeast University, said at the press conference last month.
The Post has attempted to contact Wang for additional details.
Regarding its own role, the Post asked Wen Dao this question: “Do you think AI ethical large language models will become the new internet ethics police?”
“AI ethical large models will not entirely become ‘internet ethics police’, but their capabilities in ethical risk assessment, decision support and knowledge dissemination enable them to assume roles similar to ‘ethical collaborators’ or ‘ethical supervisors’ to a certain extent,” it replied.

data.title: In wake of Hong Kong blaze, Southeast Asia’s fire hazards come under scrutiny
data.description:
Last year, Mutalib Uthman was forced to look on helplessly as his house burned to the ground and all his possessions were incinerated in a wall of flames and thick, black smoke.
The book publisher from Selangor said a faulty fuse box on the lower floor sparked the blaze inside his home in Bandar Baru Bangi. His two-storey house in the town near Putrajaya was destroyed quickly despite a neighbour trying to help with a fire extinguisher.
“The box was inside the store room, which was full of unused items,” he told This Week in Asia. “The fire fell onto some of these unused things, and it spread quickly.”
Fortunately, he and his family escaped unscathed.
Such fires are increasingly common across Southeast Asia’s densely packed urban areas, where serious structural defects and poor safety practices are common, according to analysts.

From a building fire in Vietnam’s Hanoi that killed 56 in 2023 and a casino blaze in the town of Poipet in Cambodia that left 26 dead in 2022 to shophouse infernos in the Indonesian capital of Jakarta in 2024 that killed seven, “[towns and] cities have many fire traps contained within them”, said Rosli Azad Khan, a Malaysian planning and urban expert.
“Combustible materials, such as old wooden houses and closely spaced wooden structures and asbestos roofing, allow rapid flame spread, turning a single house fire into multi-house conflagrations,” he added.
The risks of urban fires came into global focus following the inferno last week at Hong Kong’s Wang Fuk Court estate, which killed 159 people in a blaze that took nearly two days to put out.
Police have arrested 21 people, alleging that poor materials were one of the key causes of the catastrophe.
The fire spread across seven towers covered in bamboo scaffolding and protective mesh. Hong Kong’s authorities have ordered scaffold netting to be removed at about 200 locations across the city by Saturday following a safety inspection.

Barely a week goes by in Southeast Asia without reports of fires destroying homes, as safety preparedness and official oversight fail to keep pace with the development of the region’s megacities.
Often, dense alleyways, poor wiring, gas cooking in cramped lanes and single-exit buildings, combined with lax enforcement, heighten the likelihood of deadly urban fires.
“Fuse boxes are the most common cause of house fires,” a Bangkok fire inspector told This Week in Asia, requesting anonymity. “Most people don’t check them, and safety inspections are easy to get around,” he said, referring to the tendency for unscrupulous developers and officials to turn a blind eye to new electricity wires installed in old buildings.
The outcome of poor fire safety and house design can be devastating.
In the case of the 2023 fire in Hanoi, a short circuit from a parked gas-powered scooter caused flames to engulf the building.

The common “tube house” design of buildings in Vietnam, each with one exit and windows caged, poses a major fire risk as residents may not be able to escape, while rescuers could face access issues in the event of an inferno, according to safety experts.
Alex Webb, group leader and fire safety engineer at CSIRO Infrastructure Technologies, said fires happening outside buildings could overpower safety systems that were not designed to keep them out.
“Most fire-safety systems assume the fire stays inside the enclosure. When you have many high-rise buildings packed closely together, all undergoing work at the same time, the risk changes completely,” Webb said.
The decrepit state of infrastructure outside buildings could also pose a fire hazard, such as streets and service corridors with restricted access and inadequate hydrant networks.
Flagging the risk of “a compounding effect” in the event of a blaze, Rosli said slower arrival of emergency services, limited access, and a lack of hydrants or sprinkler systems could lead to “exponentially worse outcomes”.
Huge disparities in building density, enforcement rules and development planning capabilities in Southeast Asia can affect the extent to which fire risks are mitigated in a region of 700 million people.

In Singapore, where almost 80 per cent of residents live in high-rise public housing, corridor space of at least 1.2 metres is mandatory to ensure that emergency crews are not hampered by clutter and can move about to deal with fires.
Such a risk came into focus when a fire at a flat in Bukit Batok in 2023 killed a 60-year-old woman, with investigators attributing it to combustible materials cluttered along the corridor.
Many high-rise fires in flats in the Malaysian capital of Kuala Lumpur also face a similar fire risk, according to Rosli.
Building approvals and safety enforcement in Southeast Asian cities were often tainted by political interference and corruption, Rosli said.
Poor oversight had led to regional developers being allowed to cram many units in tight spaces, he added.
“The result is more people and vertical floor area packed into narrow street networks designed for lower densities, making fire a much greater risk.”

data.title: Mainland China’s billionaire count surges as BYD and Mixue bosses power wealth gains
data.description:
Mainland China’s billionaires have tightened their grip as Asia-Pacific’s wealthiest after their combined fortunes swelled to nearly US$1.8 trillion, with electric-vehicle (EV) maker BYD’s founder Wang Chuanfu and bubble tea chain Mixue’s bosses among the biggest drivers of that growth, according to a UBS report.
Backed by a booming tech sector and surging asset prices, the wealth of mainland China’s billionaires jumped 22.2 per cent year on year to US$1.77 trillion in the 12 months to early April, according to the report published on Thursday.
With 70 new entrants, mainland China’s billionaire count rose to 470 from 427 – second only to the United States globally.
Hong Kong’s billionaire population reached 76 in 2025 after five people joined the list and three dropped out. The city’s ultra-rich held US$328 billion in total wealth, up US$2 billion from last year.

Wang and Lu Xiangyang, founders of BYD – the world’s largest EV maker by units sold – recorded the tech sector’s second-biggest wealth gain after SpaceX founder Elon Musk, who topped the list.
Brothers Zhang Hongchao and Zhang Hongfu joined the ultra-rich club after Mixue raised US$444 million from its Hong Kong share sale in March. The stock has surged more than 56 per cent to HK$416.60 since its debut.
Hong Kong-based Hao Tang, a pre-listing investor in marketing software and games company AppLovin, benefited from the sharp appreciation in AppLovin’s US-traded share price, which nearly doubled this year.
The Chinese market showed the region’s greatest volatility, with 24 people falling below the billion-dollar threshold and two dying during the period, the report said.
UBS estimated that heirs in mainland China, Hong Kong and Taiwan were expected to inherit at least US$407 billion over the next 15 years. Hong Kong-based heirs were projected to receive the largest share, US$180.4 billion, while those in mainland cities like Beijing and Shanghai may inherit at least US$135.3 billion.

Globally, self-made billionaire wealth reached a record US$15.8 trillion in 2025, up 13 per cent year on year, marking the highest annual increase since 2021.
The wealth of US billionaires reached US$6.9 trillion, up 18 per cent from a year earlier. The number of US billionaires grew by 89 to 924, accounting for almost a third of the global billionaire population. Most of the newly minted billionaires came from the tech sector.
India’s billionaire wealth totalled US$888.2 billion, while Singapore’s reached US$258.8 billion.
The Swiss bank said the Asia-Pacific region had a higher proportion of female self-made billionaires than others.
More than four in 10 billionaires planned to increase exposure to emerging-market equities over the next 12 months, while one-third intended to boost investment in gold and precious metals, UBS said.
Tariffs, geopolitical conflict and policy uncertainty topped the list of worries for billionaires in the coming year.
The UBS report was based on an online survey of 87 billionaire clients conducted between July 10 and September 25 across 47 markets in Switzerland, Europe, Singapore, Hong Kong and the United States.
Asia-Pacific was the largest region being served and accounted for more than one-third of respondents, according to Amy Lo Choi-wan, chairman of UBS global wealth management Asia and CEO of UBS Hong Kong.
The technology sector is expected to continue to drive wealth creation in the region, Lo added.
“Hong Kong is seeing significant interest from our billionaire clients in new economy companies,” Lo said. “The IPO [initial public offering] market reflects this trend – technology firms dominated both recent listings and the pipeline of companies qualified for future IPOs.”
Some 90 per cent of Asia-Pacific billionaires identified technology, specifically artificial intelligence, as the top global social challenge for younger generations, according to Eva Lee, head of Greater China equities at UBS global wealth management chief investment office. Some 54 per cent of second-generation billionaires invested in hedge funds, reflecting asset-class experience from prior decades, she added.

data.title: India and Japan show impact of Trump’s tariffs extend beyond trade
data.description:
For a sign of the degree to which US President Donald Trump’s decision in August to double tariffs on most Indian goods to a staggering 50 per cent – the highest rate across Asia – is weighing on investor sentiment, look no further than the rupee.
Having plumbed a series of record lows in recent months, India’s currency has been the worst performer in Asia this year and has breached the psychologically important level of 90 per US dollar. One of the last major economies yet to strike a trade deal with the United States, India has suffered because of America’s role as its largest export market.
Even though the country’s vast domestic market is the core driver of growth, a widening current account deficit and a sharp increase in outflows of foreign capital from India’s stock market have heaped pressure on the rupee.
Yet the strain on the currency is not the only way in which Trump’s tariffs are weighing on India’s economy. Last month, Prime Minister Narendra Modi announced the most radical overhaul of India’s Byzantine labour laws since the country’s independence from Britain in 1947.
Although the legislation was approved by parliament in 2020, its implementation was delayed because of stiff opposition from labour unions. The reforms, which consolidate 29 laws into four new codes, make it easier for employers to hire and fire workers, increase job flexibility and formalise India’s vast informal sector.
Crucially, the threshold for firms requiring government approval to recruit or lay off workers, relocate or shut down has been raised from 100 to 300 workers. The changes also do away with restrictions on hiring women for certain jobs. State Bank of India expects the overhaul to increase the share of formal employment by 15 per cent, helping create 7.7 million new jobs.
While more liberalisation and deregulation are needed to sustain India’s growth rate, and unions have already begun industrial action, rewriting the country’s outdated labour laws is one of the most consequential reforms in emerging markets in recent decades.

Trump’s tariffs, moreover, served as a potent catalyst for the measures to be enforced. “The threat of slower growth due to trade disruptions prompted Prime Minister Modi to rekindle the reform programme,” said Shumita Deveshwar of TS Lombard.
An external shock ended up pushing India’s economy towards deeper structural adjustments. According to HSBC, India is one of the markets in Asia where “there are structural stories at play” and could benefit from “a pivot away from crowded [artificial intelligence] trades” as concerns about a bubble in AI-driven technology stocks intensify.
In Japan, by contrast, the consequences of Trump’s assault on the global trading system could prove more damaging. Although Tokyo struck a trade deal with Washington in July that lowered the tariff rate on Japanese goods to 15 per cent, sparing the country’s all-important car sector from higher levies, the accord was extremely favourable to the US and excluded defence spending commitments on the part of Japan.
By pressuring America’s closest ally in Asia to spend more on defence, Trump cast doubt over the depth of the US commitment to defend Japan. Partly to placate Trump, Japan’s new prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, brought forward a target of increasing defence spending to 2 per cent of economic output by two years.


However, her hawkish defence policies and pro-stimulus stance are endangering Japan’s economic and financial stability. The rise in defence spending, coupled with Takaichi’s comments suggesting that a People’s Liberation Army attack on Taiwan could be an existential crisis for Japan, has infuriated Beijing, causing an outpouring of anti-Japanese sentiment in China.
The risk of a China-induced shock to Japan’s thriving tourist industry – particularly over the longer term – coincides with mounting concern in bond markets over the additional issuance of debt to fund the increase in spending.
Japan’s bond yields have risen to their highest levels since the 2008 global financial crisis, while the yen is close to its weakest level against the US dollar since 1986. In a sign of the tensions in Japanese economic policy, the Bank of Japan is under pressure to raise interest rates this month. In a report on December 1, JPMorgan said “we see an unmanageable rise in long-term interest rates and yen depreciation as key risk factors for Sanaenomics.”

Yet while the impact of Trump’s tariffs in Asia extends beyond trade, the tariffs themselves have thrown up some surprises. This is partly because the levies did not damage supply chains and push up inflation as much as many feared.
Moreover, the AI boom has been a boon to Asia’s tech-driven economies which account for a large share of global chipmaking, AI memory and advanced packaging in the semiconductor supply chain. The AI cycle has also helped deepen intra-Asian trade, particularly Chinese exports to Southeast Asia.
Still, investors are increasingly concerned about concentration risk in Asia’s stock markets. In Taiwan, South Korea and Thailand, AI-related sectors in the MSCI equity indices for the three countries have a weighting of 86.3, 55.5 and 33.6 per cent respectively, according to data from Bank of America.
This is why India is the country to watch. As Japan struggles with acute policy dilemmas and AI bubble fears stalk markets, India looks relatively resilient and reform-minded. Provided it can reach a trade agreement with the US quickly, Asia’s third-largest economy could emerge in a stronger position next year.

data.title: Malaysia’s Penang set to scoop up Chinese tourists skipping Japan, Thailand’s Hat Yai
data.description:
With Chinese tourists shunning Japan and Thailand’s Hat Yai, Malaysia’s Penang state is poised for a year-end tourism bonanza.
Penang tourism committee chairman Wong Hong Wai said Indonesians and Singaporeans, along with Malaysians who normally prefer Hat Yai, are expected to redirect their holiday plans to the state.
“December is traditionally one of the busiest periods, coinciding with the school holidays in both Malaysia and Singapore, and the Christmas and new year festive break.
“There is already a rise in hotel occupancy and this momentum is expected to ­continue.
“We have a packed calendar of events, including the Penang Bridge International Marathon on December 14 and the Penang Chingay Parade on December 20, which always draw strong local and foreign participation,” he said.
According to reports, Chinese tourists cancelled their trips to Japan due to a worsening diplomatic row between both countries.
The boycott came following remarks by Japan’s Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi that linked the use of military force in any Taiwan conflict with the potential deployment of Japanese troops.
Wong said Penang’s blend of festivals, heritage food and outdoor attractions made it a natural beneficiary of shifting travel patterns, adding that the state currently receives 72 flights a week from seven cities in China.

Between January and September, the number of Chinese visitors rose 90.24 per cent to 161,599 from 84,947 compared with the same period last year, according to data from the Penang Immigration Department.
Malaysian Association of Hotels Penang chapter chairman Tony Goh predicted an influx of tourists from December 10 onwards as many travellers are likely to cancel their trips to Hat Yai and other regional destinations.
“In Hat Yai alone, there are 16,000 hotel rooms. On weekends, the city typically receives around 20,000 visitors from Malaysia and other countries.
“With the city still recovering from floods, Penang can capture some of this diverted tourist ­traffic,” he said.
Goh added that domestic ­travellers have turned to Penang as an alternative holiday spot, driving a noticeable pickup in hotel bookings.
“Hotels are about 40 per cent to 50 per cent booked but I expect occupancy to rise steadily by 5 per cent to 10 per cent every week until the year-end,” he said.


Hotel Neo+ Penang operations director John Kim said occupancy is expected to rise in the coming weeks, mostly through last-minute bookings.
“The year-end Hat Yai crowd may divert to Penang as recovery there will take months.
“For now, the increase is ­minimal but we expect about a 20 per cent pickup soon,” he said, ­adding that the hotel is seeing a mix of local and foreign guests.
Flamingo Hotel by the Beach general manager Ong Hai Leong said Chinese tourists often travel in large family groups, including grandparents and young children.
“Some come to explore our international schools, others ­simply to enjoy Penang’s food,” he said, adding that Chinese visitors are food-driven and plan their trips around local cuisine.
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: As China’s companies become industry leaders, where in the world are they going?
data.description:
With their domestic profits narrowing and production capacity expanding, China’s firms are continuing to widen their overseas footprints in search of new, more lucrative markets. In this series, we examine China Inc.’s next phase of “going global” and the complex, challenging international environment its companies have chosen to enter.
The “Made in China” label has evolved considerably in recent decades. Mostly found affixed to low-cost goods of relative simplicity in China’s early years as a manufacturing powerhouse, it can now be seen on a multitude of sophisticated, high-value products from an array of companies that have become formidable competitors to some of the world’s wealthiest multinational firms.
Between 2001 – when China joined the World Trade Organization after 15 years of negotiations – and 2024, the country’s value-added share of global manufacturing rocketed from 11 per cent to 33 per cent, with a hand in the production of goods across most of the world’s supply chains.
In this explainer, the Post examines the current global footprint of China Inc. as the country’s companies seek leadership roles – or have already taken them – in numerous industries, including the highly complex and technologically advanced sectors of tomorrow.
In the driver’s seat
China’s carmakers, particularly those producing electric and hybrid vehicles, have entered a new phase of global expansion.
To date, eight out of the country’s 10 largest carmakers have announced overseas investment plans this year – including factories – spanning at least 15 countries, according to calculations made by the Post.
Europe has emerged as a major arena in this global drive. BYD, China’s largest producer of electric vehicles (EVs), has confirmed plans to establish its European headquarters and a passenger car factory in Hungary. The company is also reportedly considering Spain as the leading candidate for its third European plant.
Southeast Asia, with its vast consumer base and high adoption rate for new-energy vehicles, has also become a magnet for Chinese car manufacturers.
According to a survey by Chinese technology and financial news outlet 36Kr, the region had the highest penetration rate of Chinese car brands worldwide, standing at 25.82 per cent, compared with just 0.85 per cent in North America.


Among the 11 countries in the region, Vietnam and Thailand have attracted the most investment this year. Geely signed a joint-venture agreement in Vietnam to build an assembly plant, while Chery Automobile broke ground in May on a US$319 million factory project. In Thailand, BYD’s local plant began exporting its first batch of 959 Dolphin EVs to Europe in August.
Further afield, Brazil – Latin America’s most populous country and its largest economy – has emerged as the top destination for Chinese automotive investment. BYD recently inaugurated its car manufacturing base there, the company’s largest EV plant outside Asia. GAC Motor has also announced plans to establish a local factory by the end of 2026.
Two other Chinese carmakers have followed suit. In August, Great Wall Motors started its first vehicle manufacturing plant building in Latin America, while Geely Automobile has previously unveiled plans for additional investment in the country.
Powering up
New energy and energy storage are other fields in which Chinese companies have made a sizeable foothold overseas. This year, at least five battery makers and six photovoltaics (PV) firms have announced investments in 14 countries.
Southeast Asia stands out as the brightest spot in China’s new-energy investment map. Indonesia has drawn the largest share, with battery giant CATL and solar manufacturers Longi and Trina Solar all announcing projects in the country this year.
Malaysia has also attracted attention, securing commitments from two Chinese solar firms: Eve Energy and GCL Systems. Thailand has likewise locked in factory investments from Chinese photovoltaic producers.
Around 19 publicly listed Chinese photovoltaic companies, including CATL, have so far established 24 production sites across four Southeast Asian countries, according to a May report by industry news outlet Solarbe.
Europe has emerged as another destination for Chinese clean-energy capital. Hungary, already a hub for EV battery production, has announced investment and plant construction plans from Eve and CATL. The latter company has also broken ground on a battery plant in Spain, expected to begin operations in late 2026.
Meanwhile, PV manufacturer Das Solar has announced plans to build its first overseas factory in France. Other European countries – including Slovakia, Portugal and Germany – have also welcomed new projects from Chinese new-energy companies this year.




Add to cart
China’s online retail platforms – some of which are among the world’s largest – are accelerating the development of their warehouse and logistics networks, with nodes across Europe, the Middle East, the US and Southeast Asia.
Fast-fashion giant Shein announced plans this year to lease a large warehouse in Vietnam to support both its domestic operations and export logistics.
The company has already established overseas warehouses in the US, Belgium, Spain, Australia, Indonesia, Singapore, the United Arab Emirates, India, Saudi Arabia and Italy.
In November, Shein opened its first-ever physical outlet at the BHV Le Marais department store in Paris, following explosive online sales that reached tens of millions of users across Europe.
Another major player, Temu, has deepened its logistics presence through a new partnership with Turkey’s Horoz Logistics. According to a 2025 report by Forest Shipping, Temu’s localised warehousing services now cover Germany, France, Spain, the Netherlands, Italy and Austria.
AliExpress – an online retailer under the Alibaba Group, owner of the South China Morning Post – has also expanded aggressively, establishing overseas fulfilment centres in 30 countries as of September, according to the Chinese outlet 36Kr.
Chinese consumer brands – spanning food and drink chains, toy IPs, lifestyle retailers and food-delivery platforms – are also rapidly expanding across the Americas and Southeast Asia.
Lifestyle retailer Miniso, known for its playful design collaborations, was among the early movers. Since opening its first US store in Pasadena, California, in 2017, the brand’s presence in the country has grown to more than 200 locations.
Its success helped pave the way for others. In 2024, Pop Mart captured global attention with its wildly popular Labubu figures and other toys, and now operates around 40 stores across the US, signalling the rise of Chinese pop culture on foreign shelves.


At the same time, Chinese drink brands are taking bold steps into Southeast Asia. Tea and coffee chains such as Mixue, Heytea and Luckin Coffee are expanding rapidly, drawn by the region’s 700 million strong consumer base and a market far less saturated than that of their homeland.
By the end of 2024, roughly 60 Chinese restaurant brands had opened more than 6,100 outlets across Southeast Asia – more than triple the 1,800 stores recorded in 2022, according to Singapore-based consultancy Momentum Works.
China’s tech-driven service platforms have also made forays abroad. Food-delivery giant Meituan has launched its offshore brand Keeta in the outskirts of Sao Paulo, pledging to invest US$1.1 billion in its Brazilian operations over the next five years. It has already set up shop in Hong Kong and the Middle East.
Meanwhile, ride-hailing firm Didi Chuxing plans to double its investment in the South American country to US$37.7 million by 2026, expanding its food-delivery arm, 99Food, through temporary fee cuts aimed at attracting partner restaurants.
Press play
Beyond more tangible goods, China’s digital platforms are attracting a global audience – including in the US, where TikTok has become a cultural phenomenon among younger generations.
Another breakout hit is ReelShort, a platform launched by Crazy Maple Studio in Silicon Valley, a company backed in part by Beijing-based digital publisher COL Group. With a line-up of “vertical dramas” – series with ultra-short episodes and heightened storylines – it has drawn over 55 million monthly active users and climbed to the top three on the entertainment chart of Apple’s US App Store in November.
Now, ReelShort is taking its formula global. In Latin America, the company has partnered with Colombia’s Caracol Television to license content and produce original Spanish-language dramas in its signature style – a move aimed at capturing one of the world’s fastest-growing digital audiences.
